title: Weather & Seasons
shortSummary: Discuss the weather and Japan's four distinct seasons, a favourite topic of small talk.
culturalNote: The four seasons (shiki) are central to Japanese culture, and commenting on the weather is the most natural way to open a conversation or a letter. Spring brings cherry blossoms (sakura) and the custom of hanami, picnicking beneath the blooms. The rainy season (tsuyu) in early summer is followed by hot, humid days, then a colourful autumn of red maple leaves (momiji), and a crisp, dry winter. Seasonal awareness shapes food, festivals, and even greetings: a letter might begin by remarking on the warmth of spring or the chill of late autumn. The phrase ii tenki desu ne ('nice weather, isn't it?') with the sentence-ending ne invites agreement and is a quintessential piece of Japanese small talk that builds rapport.

grammar.explanation: Many weather words are i-adjectives ending in -i: atsui (hot), samui (cold), suzushii (cool). They go directly before desu in the polite form, as in atsui desu. To make the past, change -i to -katta: atsukatta desu ('it was hot').

examples.native: 昨日は寒かったです。(kinou wa samukatta desu)

grammar.commonMistake: Forming the past as samui deshita; the correct past of an i-adjective is samukatta desu.

grammar.explanation: The sentence-ending particle ne softens a statement and invites the listener to agree, like the English tag 'isn't it?'. Ii tenki desu ne means 'nice weather, isn't it?'. It is the heart of friendly Japanese small talk.

examples.native: 今日は暑いですね。(kyou wa atsui desu ne)
桜がきれいですね。(sakura ga kirei desu ne)
examples.en: The cherry blossoms are pretty, aren't they?

grammar.commonMistake: Overusing ne in formal writing; it belongs in conversational, rapport-building speech.

dialogue.setup: Two neighbours, Hana and Tom, chat outside on a spring morning.

lines.speaker: ハナ
lines.native: おはようございます。いい天気ですね。
lines.transliteration: ohayou gozaimasu. ii tenki desu ne
lines.english: Good morning. Nice weather, isn't it?

lines.speaker: トム
lines.native: そうですね。桜がきれいです。
lines.transliteration: sou desu ne. sakura ga kirei desu
lines.english: Yes, it is. The cherry blossoms are beautiful.

lines.speaker: トム
lines.native: はい、大好きです。でも夏は蒸し暑いです。
lines.transliteration: hai, daisuki desu. demo natsu wa mushiatsui desu
lines.english: Yes, I love it. But summer is humid.

lines.speaker: ハナ
lines.native: 本当に。明日は雨が降ります。
lines.transliteration: hontou ni. ashita wa ame ga furimasu

lines.speaker: トム
lines.native: じゃあ、傘が必要ですね。
lines.transliteration: jaa, kasa ga hitsuyou desu ne
lines.english: Then an umbrella will be needed, won't it?

lines.speaker: ハナ
lines.native: そうですね。気をつけてください。
lines.transliteration: sou desu ne. ki o tsukete kudasai

quiz.question: How do you say 'It's hot today' in Japanese?
quiz.correctAnswer: 今日は暑いです (kyou wa atsui desu)
quiz.explanation: Atsui (hot) is an i-adjective placed directly before desu.

quiz.question: Which particle invites the listener to agree, like 'isn't it?'
quiz.explanation: Ne softens a statement and seeks agreement, central to small talk.

quiz.question: 昨日は寒 _____ です。(It was cold yesterday)
quiz.explanation: An i-adjective forms its past by changing -i to -katta: samukatta desu.

quiz.question: What is hanami?
quiz.explanation: Hanami is the spring custom of viewing and picnicking beneath sakura blooms.

quiz.question: Arrange: ですね / 天気 / いい (nice weather, isn't it?)
quiz.correctAnswer: いい天気ですね (ii tenki desu ne)
quiz.explanation: Ii (good) + tenki (weather) + desu ne is the classic small-talk phrase.
